skill.name: data-lake-architect
skill.description: Guides data lake and lakehouse architecture decisions for storage layout, partitioning, file sizing, schema design, retention, and table format selection.
skill.summary: Design scalable data lakes with practical lakehouse architecture guidance.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found Markdown examples and data architecture terminology only. No executable commands, network calls, filesystem writes, secret access, or prompt injection attempts were found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby shell backtick findings are Markdown code fences and architecture examples. The skill only declares Read, Grep, and Glob tools, and I found no instruction that executes shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are fenced Markdown blocks used for diagrams, examples, and explanatory text. No shell execution primitive or instruction to run external commands appears in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hash Partitioning Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits are data architecture references such as hash partitioning and lakehouse terminology. They are not cryptographic implementations and do not handle secrets or authentication.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context uses hash only for data distribution guidance, not for password storage, signatures, encryption, or integrity checks. The surrounding text is architectural guidance rather than executable security code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Architecture Text Flagged as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance hits are benign recommendations about data layout, nesting depth, and partition strategy. I found no commands or instructions to enumerate host, network, process, or credential information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text concerns data lake partitioning and query ergonomics. It does not ask the assistant to inspect system state or collect environment information.

content.value_statement: Large data lakes can become slow, expensive, and hard to govern without clear architecture choices. This skill guides partitioning, storage layout, schema evolution, table formats, retention, and cost-aware lakehouse design.
actual_capabilities: Recommends raw, processed, and curated storage layouts for data lakes.
Chooses partitioning strategies based on query patterns, data volume, and cardinality.
Explains file sizing, compaction, compression, and row group trade-offs.
Compares raw Parquet and Iceberg for schema evolution and operational needs.
Reviews architecture against storage, partitioning, performance, operations, and cost checklists.
Provides practical guidance for retention tiers and lifecycle policies.
limitations: Does not deploy cloud infrastructure or modify storage systems directly.
Does not run benchmark tests against a live data lake.
Requires user-provided workload, query, and volume details for precise recommendations.
Focuses on architecture guidance, not full application implementation.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Lakehouse
use_cases.description: Design a layered storage model, table format choice, and partitioning approach before ingestion starts.

use_cases.title: Fix Slow Analytical Queries
use_cases.description: Review file sizes, partition granularity, compression, and metadata design to reduce query latency.

use_cases.title: Control Data Lake Costs
use_cases.description: Create retention tiers, compression choices, and compaction practices for lower storage and scan costs.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Layout
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an existing folder structure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this data lake layout and suggest improvements for raw, processed, and curated zones. Ask for any missing details first.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Partitions
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate partition planning for analytical tables.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose partition columns for a table with these query patterns, daily volume, and cardinality details. Explain the trade-offs.

prompt_templates.title: Select a Table Format
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced decision between Parquet and Iceberg.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare raw Parquet and Iceberg for my workload. Consider schema evolution, concurrent writers, update needs, metadata cost, and team operations.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Lakehouse Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this lakehouse architecture for scalability, cost, retention, compaction, schema evolution, and query performance. Return prioritized recommendations.

output_examples.input: We ingest 500 GB of events daily and query by date and event type.
output_examples.output: Use daily time partitions first, then consider event type only if cardinality is controlled and filters are consistent. Target 100 MB to 1 GB files and monitor small file growth.

output_examples.input: Our schema changes often and several services write to the same table.
output_examples.output: Prefer Iceberg because it supports schema evolution, concurrent writes, snapshot isolation, and partition evolution. Budget for metadata operations and operational learning.

output_examples.input: Our data lake has many tiny files and slow queries.
output_examples.output: Prioritize compaction, larger writer batches, and partition simplification. Check whether hourly partitions can become daily partitions without hurting common queries.

best_practices: Start with query patterns, data volume, and retention needs before choosing partitions.
Keep raw data immutable so pipelines can reprocess history when logic changes.
Use file size, compression, and compaction targets as operational requirements.
anti_patterns: Do not partition by high-cardinality fields unless a clear bucketing strategy exists.
Do not mix raw source data and curated business tables in the same storage zone.
Do not choose Iceberg only for trend alignment without operational ownership.

faq.question: Can this skill design a complete lakehouse architecture?
faq.answer: Yes. It can propose storage zones, table formats, partitioning, retention tiers, and performance practices from your requirements.

faq.question: Does it support both Parquet and Iceberg guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains when raw Parquet is enough and when Iceberg provides better operational behavior.

faq.question: Can it optimize an existing data lake?
faq.answer: Yes. Share the current layout, table sizes, query patterns, and pain points for a focused review.

faq.question: Does it execute commands or change data?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides architecture guidance and uses read-only analysis tools.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide daily data volume, query filters, latency goals, schema change frequency, retention rules, and storage platform.

faq.question: Is this only for Rust data engineering?
faq.answer: No. It references Rust examples, but the architecture guidance applies to general cloud data lakes.
